definition: Cross-asset markets are pricing an internally inconsistent labor market narrative. Equities (S&P ~21x forward P/E) price robust consumption at ~4.0% unemployment. Rates (pricing 75-100bp of easing) price enough softening for Fed cuts. These are arithmetically inconsistent without an exogenous positive supply shock. Reverse DCF shows current valuations require either above-target inflation or heroic productivity.
evidence: No other agent directly contradicted this observation
Maturity-dependent correlation bifurcation: 2Y negative, 30Y positive as fiscal-dominance fingerprint
Credit spreads embed disinflationary glide path inconsistent with breakeven term structure
IG OAS at 90-110bp and HY at 300-350bp embed 65-70% disinflationary probability inconsistent with structural persistence
S&P 21-22x forward P/E prices ~4.0% UE + robust consumption; 50-75bp easing priced requires softening; HY OAS 300-350bp prices no deterioration
Persistence base rate from 6 completed episodes since 1990: median ~13 months
ERP requires both tight labor (consumption) and loose labor (rate cuts) — arithmetically inconsistent
Immigration reversal removes the supply-side channel that was the only mechanism for reconciling all three markets simultaneously
open_questions: Does passive investing (~50% AUM) extend the repricing lag to 36-60 months?
Which asset class will resolve the inconsistency?
Is the inconsistency exploitable given transaction costs and timing uncertainty?
Which asset class is most mispriced relative to the likely inflation regime?
How should the corridor of instability framework be operationalized for portfolio construction?
Which asset class will reprice — equities, rates, or credit?
Is there a novel mechanism (AI productivity, fiscal expansion) that could reconcile the inconsistency without repricing?
Does the resolution direction follow any predictable pattern from observables?
